romania.title: Google vrea să elibereze 64 de milioane de țânțari. Motivul operațiunii
romania.summary: Gigantul american din domeniul tehnologiei Google a trimis o cerere către Agenția americană pentru Protecția Mediului (EPA) în care susține că vrea să elibereze 64 de milioane de țânțari infestați cu o anumită bacterie în California și Florida, în cadrul unui experiment care, în mod contraintuitiv, ar putea reduce populațiile de țânțari ce transmit boli, …

romania.title: StartupCafe.ro: Business CheckIn. Ana-Maria era încă studentă când a pornit propria ei afacere. „Acum suntem la peste 2000 de proiecte”
romania.summary: Ana-Maria Rapițeanu (foto) este arhitect și fondatoarea 3DVizion, business lansat în 2015, specializat în vizualizare arhitecturală și design interior implementabil. Firma ajută dezvoltatorii, agențiile imobiliare și cumpărătorii să își imagineze cum va arăta un spațiu înainte ca acesta să fie amenajat sau renovat. După peste 2.000 de proiecte realizate în România și în străinătate, antreprenoarea își …

romania.title: Miracol pe Everest: un ghid considerat mort a fost găsit în viață după șase zile la peste 7.500 de metri altitudine
romania.summary: Un ghid montan nepalez dat dispărut și considerat mort după o ascensiune pe Everest a fost găsit în viață la șase zile de la dispariție. Bărbatul, în vârstă de 52 de ani, a fost descoperit coborând spre Tabăra de Bază, în condiții în care șansele de supraviețuire erau considerate minime.

romania.title: 😂 Previzibil! După ce ieri a fost Ziua Bicicletei, azi celebrăm Ziua Durerii de Cur
romania.summary: Dacă ieri am avut Ziua Mondială a Bicicletei, era absolut obligatoriu ca astăzi să celebrăm Ziua Durerii de Cur. Chiar și dacă nu am fi avut azi o zi oficială a durerii de cur, tot am fi avut azi un record de oameni care au dureri de cur. Durerea resimțită în zona feselor a doua […] The post Previzibil! După ce ieri a fost Ziua Bicicletei, azi celebrăm Ziua Durerii de Cur appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Socului Kebap, contract de 174 de milioane € din SAFE pentru hrănirea generalilor români
romania.summary: Veste excelentă venită dinspre Ministerul Apărării: o sumă importantă din pachetul SAFE pentru înzestrarea armatei române va ajunge la o firmă din România! Este vorba de Socului Kebap, care s-a ales cu un contract de 174 de milioane de euro, bani pentru care va trebui să asigure hrănirea generalilor români. Generalii Armatei Române au optat […] The post Socului Kebap, contract de 174 de milioane € din SAFE pentru hrănirea generalilor români appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Şoşoacă, fraţii Tate şi Steven Seagal participă la Concursul de Frumuseţe de la Sankt Petersburg
romania.summary: Indiferent că e război sau pace, ce-i frumos şi lui Dumnezeu îi place! „Las Vegasul Rusiei”, Sankt Petersburg, găzduieşte în această perioadă Concursul Internaţional de Frumuseţe Ivan Cel Groaznic 2026, la care sunt aşteptaţi să participe unii dintre cei mai sexy oameni în viaţă. Printre personalităţile care şi-au anunţat prezenţa la „Davosul frumuseţii” se numără […] The post Şoşoacă, fraţii Tate şi Steven Seagal participă la Concursul de Frumuseţe de la Sankt Petersburg appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
romania.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

medical.title: Addressing ACL injuries in women: Expert explains prevention and treatment
medical.summary: No one wants to hear the distinctive pop of an ACL tearing or rupturing, which typically means the player's season is at an end. The ACL, or anterior cruciate ligament, is located inside the knee and provides stability during movements such as cutting, turning, twisting, and jumping. Deanna Brinks, M.D., a physical medicine and rehabilitation and sports medicine physician at the Mayo Clinic Health System in Mankato, explains prevention and treatment.

medical.title: After early pregnancy loss, 'what if' thinking affects 72% within first week
medical.summary: When a person goes through a traumatic experience, they often find themselves thinking that what happened could have been different or even avoided. This process, known as counterfactual thinking, is an automatic psychological response to adverse events. Now, a study published in the journal Scientific Reports explores how this type of thinking affects women who have suffered an early pregnancy loss.

medical.title: Heart rhythm monitoring with a smartphone could save health care resources
medical.summary: Smartphone-based heart rhythm monitoring from home can reduce same-day cancellations and help save significant health care resources ahead of planned electrical cardioversion in patients with atrial fibrillation. This is shown in a new study published in JAMA Cardiology by researchers at Karolinska Institutet and Danderyd Hospital.

medical.title: Alzheimer's gene map expands to 91 loci, revealing 16 previously unknown risk regions
medical.summary: An international collaboration of genetic researchers has identified more than 90 genetic regions associated with the risk of Alzheimer's disease and related dementias. The large-scale meta-analysis reveals new biological insights into the disease, highlighting the important roles of immune processes, beta-amyloid and tau biology, and lipid metabolism.

medical.title: How cells fight infection from the inside: Newly identified ADX pathway may broaden understanding of immunity
medical.summary: When thinking of the immune system, most people imagine white blood cells putting up a fight against invading germs in the bloodstream. But now, in research published in Molecular Cell, scientists detail a separate but equally important route by which our bodies fight infection—directly inside already infected cells.

medical.title: Older LGBTQ+ adults fear less support as they age, poll shows
medical.summary: LGBTQ+ people are woven into the lives of middle-aged and older Americans, a new national poll reports. About seven out of 10 non-LGBTQ+ people older than 50 (69%) have at least one personal connection to a person who is LGBTQ+, according to results from the University of Michigan's National Poll on Healthy Aging.

medical.title: Motherhood leaves lifelong brain marks via dopamine-linked epigenetic switch, mouse study suggests
medical.summary: Becoming a mother changes the brain not just temporarily, but for life. Pregnancy and the postpartum period trigger lasting changes in the maternal brain through the brain chemical dopamine, producing long-term benefits to learning, memory, and maternal behavior, a process disrupted by chronic postpartum stress.

medical.title: Online type 2 diabetes support linked to better health outcomes in England
medical.summary: A free online NHS program is delivering meaningful health improvements for adults living with type 2 diabetes (T2D) across England, a new study by University of Manchester researchers has shown. The NHS program called "Healthy Living for people with type 2 diabetes" is a website containing written articles, videos, self-assessment quizzes and tools.

medical.title: What we still get wrong about how people from non‑Western backgrounds recover from trauma
medical.summary: Over the past few decades, researchers have developed effective treatments for post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), a psychiatric disorder some people develop after experiencing trauma. These treatments often involve talking through the trauma and understanding what happened with a therapist.

medical.title: A study of 8,300 older adults revealed a surprising salt habit
medical.summary: A large study of older adults in Brazil found that adding extra salt at the table is still a common habit, especially among men. While too much salt is linked to serious health problems and faster cognitive decline, researchers discovered that women’s salt-shaking habits were tied more closely to lifestyle and diet.

medical.title: How common is sex‑selective abortion in Australia, really?
medical.summary: New South Wales parliament is debating a bill this week that seeks to ban abortions performed on the basis of fetal sex.

medical.title: Radical change can lead to a fairer and greener world, says new report
medical.summary: A new study argues that rising living standards, shorter working hours and a liveable climate are achievable, if the world tackles extreme inequality The post Radical change can lead to a fairer and greener world, says new report appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Smartphone unlock can measure heart rate, potentially bringing health monitoring to billions worldwide
medical.summary: Wearable devices like smartwatches and fitness trackers have revolutionized the way we monitor our health. Worn around the clock, these devices quietly collect valuable data—from heart rate and blood oxygen levels to sleep quality—giving users a real-time window into their well-being without disrupting their daily lives.

medical.title: Irregular sleep in preschool-age children is associated with lower verbal and memory performance
medical.summary: A new study to be presented at the SLEEP 2026 annual meeting found that irregular sleep—including variability in sleep timing and duration—was associated with lower receptive vocabulary and visuospatial memory scores in preschool-age children, even after accounting for total sleep duration.

medical.title: Newfound 'switchboard' helps brain form new memories without forgetting older ones
medical.summary: The brain may reuse some cells to store many different memories without mixing them up with or erasing older memories, a new study in mice suggests. Led by NYU Langone Health researchers, the study revealed that about 1 in 4 memory cells in a brain area called the hippocampus acts as a shared "hub" that links incoming and outgoing signals. A report on the findings was published in the journal Nature.

medical.title: Poor sleep health in adolescence linked to hospitalization and worse health in young adulthood
medical.summary: A new study to be presented at the SLEEP 2026 annual meeting found that poorer sleep health according to wrist actigraphy and insomnia symptoms during adolescence predicted a higher likelihood of an overnight hospital stay during young adulthood around seven years later, even after accounting for adolescent hospitalizations and sleep health.

medical.title: Scientists finally crack an “undruggable” pancreatic cancer target and nearly double survival
medical.summary: For decades, pancreatic cancer has been one of the most lethal cancers, with few effective treatment options. A new drug, daraxonrasib, targets the KRAS mutation that fuels most pancreatic tumors—something many scientists once thought couldn't be done. In a major clinical trial, the treatment nearly doubled survival for patients with advanced disease and reduced the risk of death by 60%.

medical.title: Scientists discover the master clock that controls biological growth and development
medical.summary: A newly discovered genetic clock acts as the master timekeeper for development, orchestrating crucial bursts of gene activity throughout a worm’s growth. When the clock is disrupted, development stops, offering fresh clues about how growth-related disorders may arise.

medical.title: What went right this week: life-changing inventions, plus more
medical.summary: New life-changing inventions, a global triumph for renewables, and England’s first Dutch-style ‘cycle street’, plus more The post What went right this week: life-changing inventions, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Scientists discover why Ozempic and Wegovy weight loss eventually plateaus
medical.summary: New NIH research reveals that semaglutide sparks different responses inside appetite-controlling brain cells, offering fresh insight into why GLP-1 weight-loss drugs don’t work the same for everyone. Scientists also found a possible way to extend the drugs’ effects, potentially helping patients push past weight-loss plateaus.

medical.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
medical.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

science.title: Jupiter bow shock reveals electrons accelerating to relativistic speeds
science.summary: Electrons around Jupiter have been caught in the process of being accelerated, revealing a potentially unified mechanism for particle acceleration. The findings, published in Nature, may help constrain how energetic particles are produced throughout the universe.

science.title: Understanding Earth's hidden east-west symmetry could improve climate models
science.summary: Earth is divided into two halves: the Northern and Southern Hemispheres. Both reflect equal amounts of sunlight (albedo) even though they have different landmasses and weather patterns, especially cloud distribution. Why this is so is an ongoing mystery waiting to be solved.

science.title: Home-sorted recycling produces higher-quality plastic bales with fewer contaminants, finds study
science.summary: The quality of recycled plastic tends to be higher when the waste is pre-sorted by households compared with plastics recovered from mixed waste at a recycling facility in the Netherlands, research in Nature suggests. While mixed waste (not pre-sorted) collections may increase overall recycling volumes, this comes at the cost of reduced quality of the recycled product. The findings highlight the need to improve recycling collection and sorting systems.

science.title: Can fighting via text be good for a relationship?
science.summary: Today, many of our social interactions are routed through technology: text messages, video calls, voice messages, emails and instant messaging apps. In romantic relationships, couples often use these methods to deal with conflicts.

science.title: How tuning atomic order and surface chemistry can shape MXenes
science.summary: Scientists at the U.S. Department of Energy's (DOE) Argonne National Laboratory are helping show what it means to design a material almost atom-by-atom. In two publications, scientists show they can carefully choose the types of atoms in a material, where those atoms sit and what is attached to the surfaces of its atom-thin layers.

science.title: DigMethpy: An AI-driven platform for accelerating methane pyrolysis catalyst discovery
science.summary: Researchers have developed a new artificial intelligence-powered platform that could significantly speed up the discovery of catalysts for methane pyrolysis, a promising technology for producing hydrogen with lower carbon emissions.

science.title: D&D-seq maps DNA-protein interactions in single cells with multi-omics compatibility
science.summary: A new technology allows scientists to map, in single cells, the DNA binding sites of transcription factors and other regulatory proteins that control gene activity, according to a study led by investigators at Weill Cornell Medicine and the New York Genome Center. With key advantages over methods currently in use, the technology is expected to be a powerful addition to biologists' toolkit for studying cells in health and disease.

science.title: After 20 years, scientists finally shrink a powerful laser onto a chip
science.summary: Researchers at EPFL have developed a chip-scale ultrafast laser that performs on par with traditional tabletop femtosecond lasers. The innovation could make advanced laser technologies far smaller, cheaper, and more accessible for applications ranging from medical diagnostics to atomic clocks.

science.title: What is Godzilla El Niño?
science.summary: You may have heard the rumors of a "monster El Niño." It's not the first time we've heard forecasts like this in Australia, but this time, they aren't coming out of nowhere. Early signs in the Pacific have been building for months and forecasts now point to a high likelihood of a moderate to strong El Niño developing in 2026.

science.title: Quantum shell structure reveals new rule for proton-neutron pairing inside nuclei
science.summary: Nuclear physicists used a little magic in their latest experiment conducted at the U.S. Department of Energy's Thomas Jefferson National Accelerator Facility, and the result has revealed surprising new information about the behavior of protons and neutrons inside the atom's nucleus. Specifically, the research revealed another requirement that determines how protons and neutrons pair up.

science.title: Meteors are 'extremely common.' What makes the one over New England 'rare'?
science.summary: A sonic boom shook Boston and the larger New England area with the force of 230 tons of TNT. The source came from outer space. The Saturday afternoon event triggered shock waves online, with thousands inquiring what could have caused houses to shake and a sound heard as far away as New Hampshire and Rhode Island.

science.title: Hidden in plain sight: The race to discover new species before they're gone
science.summary: When most people imagine scientists discovering new species, they probably still picture an expedition into the unknown.

science.title: Milky Way black hole's missing wind finally found after a half-century-long search
science.summary: The hunt is over. After more than 50 years of searching, astrophysicists at Northwestern University have finally discovered evidence of a powerful wind blowing from the Milky Way's central supermassive black hole, Sagittarius A* (Sgr A*).

science.title: An unfinished reckoning with police violence: Community data show ongoing systemic racism
science.summary: It's been roughly six years since the killing of George Floyd in Minneapolis sparked a global conversation about anti-Black police violence and the excessive use of police force against Black and Indigenous communities.

science.title: Laser 'origami' could help astronauts build structures on the moon
science.summary: University of Florida researchers are exploring how lasers could help astronauts build structures on the moon using materials already available there, including lunar soil transformed into glass. The work, led by Victoria M. Miller, Ph.D., an associate professor in the Herbert Wertheim College of Engineering and researcher with the UF Astraeus Space Institute, recently completed a research phase focused on laser forming, a manufacturing process that bends materials without physical contact.

science.title: ExoMars rover targets vast bed of clay in search for life
science.summary: In the region where the ExoMars Rosalind Franklin rover will search for signs of life, clay deposits extend beyond previous estimates, a new study finds. One hypothesis even suggests a vast ocean once covered the landing site.

science.title: Endangered basking sharks rely on the ocean twilight zone during long-distance migrations
science.summary: Endangered basking sharks aren't fasting during long-distance migrations. A new study led by the Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution shows that they may be foraging along the way, and in much deeper areas of the ocean than previously thought. As filter feeders, this species is most often observed close to the surface, especially in waters off of New England, but data show markedly different behavior during their winter migrations to the Sargasso Sea and the Caribbean.

science.title: Majestic manta rays dive deep to survive storm events, data reveal
science.summary: New research led by the University of the Sunshine Coast has found that reef manta rays are diving deep in storm events to find food and stay alive. As World Environment Day is celebrated around the globe on June 5, the findings offer hope for the future of a species listed as vulnerable to extinction.

science.title: Short videos may hinder learning by fragmenting attention and memory, study finds
science.summary: Recent technological advances and the introduction of new digital media platforms have dramatically changed how people learn and source information about topics that interest them. Some recent studies have found that while browsing online or scrolling down social media platforms, users tend to spend under one minute on average on individual videos.

science.title: Goethe never knew this 40-million-year-old ant was hidden in his collection
science.summary: Scientists examining amber from Goethe’s personal collection discovered three hidden fossil insects, including an extinct ant preserved in extraordinary detail. Advanced 3D imaging allowed researchers to see not only the ant’s outer features but also structures inside its body. The findings offer new clues about the species’ biology and suggest it likely built large nests in trees.

science.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
science.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? The findings highlight the need to improve recycling collection and sorting systems.

mix_items.title: After early pregnancy loss, 'what if' thinking affects 72% within first week
mix_items.summary: When a person goes through a traumatic experience, they often find themselves thinking that what happened could have been different or even avoided. This process, known as counterfactual thinking, is an automatic psychological response to adverse events. Now, a study published in the journal Scientific Reports explores how this type of thinking affects women who have suffered an early pregnancy loss.

mix_items.title: Can fighting via text be good for a relationship?
mix_items.summary: Today, many of our social interactions are routed through technology: text messages, video calls, voice messages, emails and instant messaging apps. In romantic relationships, couples often use these methods to deal with conflicts.

mix_items.title: Heart rhythm monitoring with a smartphone could save health care resources
mix_items.summary: Smartphone-based heart rhythm monitoring from home can reduce same-day cancellations and help save significant health care resources ahead of planned electrical cardioversion in patients with atrial fibrillation. This is shown in a new study published in JAMA Cardiology by researchers at Karolinska Institutet and Danderyd Hospital.

mix_items.title: How tuning atomic order and surface chemistry can shape MXenes
mix_items.summary: Scientists at the U.S. Department of Energy's (DOE) Argonne National Laboratory are helping show what it means to design a material almost atom-by-atom. In two publications, scientists show they can carefully choose the types of atoms in a material, where those atoms sit and what is attached to the surfaces of its atom-thin layers.

mix_items.title: Alzheimer's gene map expands to 91 loci, revealing 16 previously unknown risk regions
mix_items.summary: An international collaboration of genetic researchers has identified more than 90 genetic regions associated with the risk of Alzheimer's disease and related dementias. The large-scale meta-analysis reveals new biological insights into the disease, highlighting the important roles of immune processes, beta-amyloid and tau biology, and lipid metabolism.

mix_items.title: How cells fight infection from the inside: Newly identified ADX pathway may broaden understanding of immunity
mix_items.summary: When thinking of the immune system, most people imagine white blood cells putting up a fight against invading germs in the bloodstream. But now, in research published in Molecular Cell, scientists detail a separate but equally important route by which our bodies fight infection—directly inside already infected cells.

mix_items.title: DigMethpy: An AI-driven platform for accelerating methane pyrolysis catalyst discovery
mix_items.summary: Researchers have developed a new artificial intelligence-powered platform that could significantly speed up the discovery of catalysts for methane pyrolysis, a promising technology for producing hydrogen with lower carbon emissions.

mix_items.title: Older LGBTQ+ adults fear less support as they age, poll shows
mix_items.summary: LGBTQ+ people are woven into the lives of middle-aged and older Americans, a new national poll reports. About seven out of 10 non-LGBTQ+ people older than 50 (69%) have at least one personal connection to a person who is LGBTQ+, according to results from the University of Michigan's National Poll on Healthy Aging.

mix_items.title: D&D-seq maps DNA-protein interactions in single cells with multi-omics compatibility
mix_items.summary: A new technology allows scientists to map, in single cells, the DNA binding sites of transcription factors and other regulatory proteins that control gene activity, according to a study led by investigators at Weill Cornell Medicine and the New York Genome Center. With key advantages over methods currently in use, the technology is expected to be a powerful addition to biologists' toolkit for studying cells in health and disease.

mix_items.title: After 20 years, scientists finally shrink a powerful laser onto a chip
mix_items.summary: Researchers at EPFL have developed a chip-scale ultrafast laser that performs on par with traditional tabletop femtosecond lasers. The innovation could make advanced laser technologies far smaller, cheaper, and more accessible for applications ranging from medical diagnostics to atomic clocks.

mix_items.title: What is Godzilla El Niño?
mix_items.summary: You may have heard the rumors of a "monster El Niño." It's not the first time we've heard forecasts like this in Australia, but this time, they aren't coming out of nowhere. Early signs in the Pacific have been building for months and forecasts now point to a high likelihood of a moderate to strong El Niño developing in 2026.

mix_items.title: Motherhood leaves lifelong brain marks via dopamine-linked epigenetic switch, mouse study suggests
mix_items.summary: Becoming a mother changes the brain not just temporarily, but for life. Pregnancy and the postpartum period trigger lasting changes in the maternal brain through the brain chemical dopamine, producing long-term benefits to learning, memory, and maternal behavior, a process disrupted by chronic postpartum stress.

mix_items.title: Online type 2 diabetes support linked to better health outcomes in England
mix_items.summary: A free online NHS program is delivering meaningful health improvements for adults living with type 2 diabetes (T2D) across England, a new study by University of Manchester researchers has shown. The NHS program called "Healthy Living for people with type 2 diabetes" is a website containing written articles, videos, self-assessment quizzes and tools.

mix_items.title: Quantum shell structure reveals new rule for proton-neutron pairing inside nuclei
mix_items.summary: Nuclear physicists used a little magic in their latest experiment conducted at the U.S. Department of Energy's Thomas Jefferson National Accelerator Facility, and the result has revealed surprising new information about the behavior of protons and neutrons inside the atom's nucleus. Specifically, the research revealed another requirement that determines how protons and neutrons pair up.

mix_items.title: What we still get wrong about how people from non‑Western backgrounds recover from trauma
mix_items.summary: Over the past few decades, researchers have developed effective treatments for post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), a psychiatric disorder some people develop after experiencing trauma. These treatments often involve talking through the trauma and understanding what happened with a therapist.

mix_items.title: Meteors are 'extremely common.' What makes the one over New England 'rare'?
mix_items.summary: A sonic boom shook Boston and the larger New England area with the force of 230 tons of TNT. The source came from outer space. The Saturday afternoon event triggered shock waves online, with thousands inquiring what could have caused houses to shake and a sound heard as far away as New Hampshire and Rhode Island.

mix_items.title: A study of 8,300 older adults revealed a surprising salt habit
mix_items.summary: A large study of older adults in Brazil found that adding extra salt at the table is still a common habit, especially among men. While too much salt is linked to serious health problems and faster cognitive decline, researchers discovered that women’s salt-shaking habits were tied more closely to lifestyle and diet.

mix_items.title: Hidden in plain sight: The race to discover new species before they're gone
mix_items.summary: When most people imagine scientists discovering new species, they probably still picture an expedition into the unknown.

mix_items.title: How common is sex‑selective abortion in Australia, really?
mix_items.summary: New South Wales parliament is debating a bill this week that seeks to ban abortions performed on the basis of fetal sex.

mix_items.title: Milky Way black hole's missing wind finally found after a half-century-long search
mix_items.summary: The hunt is over. After more than 50 years of searching, astrophysicists at Northwestern University have finally discovered evidence of a powerful wind blowing from the Milky Way's central supermassive black hole, Sagittarius A* (Sgr A*).

mix_items.title: Radical change can lead to a fairer and greener world, says new report
mix_items.summary: A new study argues that rising living standards, shorter working hours and a liveable climate are achievable, if the world tackles extreme inequality The post Radical change can lead to a fairer and greener world, says new report appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: An unfinished reckoning with police violence: Community data show ongoing systemic racism
mix_items.summary: It's been roughly six years since the killing of George Floyd in Minneapolis sparked a global conversation about anti-Black police violence and the excessive use of police force against Black and Indigenous communities.

mix_items.title: Laser 'origami' could help astronauts build structures on the moon
mix_items.summary: University of Florida researchers are exploring how lasers could help astronauts build structures on the moon using materials already available there, including lunar soil transformed into glass. The work, led by Victoria M. Miller, Ph.D., an associate professor in the Herbert Wertheim College of Engineering and researcher with the UF Astraeus Space Institute, recently completed a research phase focused on laser forming, a manufacturing process that bends materials without physical contact.

mix_items.title: Smartphone unlock can measure heart rate, potentially bringing health monitoring to billions worldwide
mix_items.summary: Wearable devices like smartwatches and fitness trackers have revolutionized the way we monitor our health. Worn around the clock, these devices quietly collect valuable data—from heart rate and blood oxygen levels to sleep quality—giving users a real-time window into their well-being without disrupting their daily lives.

mix_items.title: ExoMars rover targets vast bed of clay in search for life
mix_items.summary: In the region where the ExoMars Rosalind Franklin rover will search for signs of life, clay deposits extend beyond previous estimates, a new study finds. One hypothesis even suggests a vast ocean once covered the landing site.

mix_items.title: Irregular sleep in preschool-age children is associated with lower verbal and memory performance
mix_items.summary: A new study to be presented at the SLEEP 2026 annual meeting found that irregular sleep—including variability in sleep timing and duration—was associated with lower receptive vocabulary and visuospatial memory scores in preschool-age children, even after accounting for total sleep duration.

mix_items.title: Endangered basking sharks rely on the ocean twilight zone during long-distance migrations
mix_items.summary: Endangered basking sharks aren't fasting during long-distance migrations. A new study led by the Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution shows that they may be foraging along the way, and in much deeper areas of the ocean than previously thought. As filter feeders, this species is most often observed close to the surface, especially in waters off of New England, but data show markedly different behavior during their winter migrations to the Sargasso Sea and the Caribbean.

mix_items.title: Newfound 'switchboard' helps brain form new memories without forgetting older ones
mix_items.summary: The brain may reuse some cells to store many different memories without mixing them up with or erasing older memories, a new study in mice suggests. Led by NYU Langone Health researchers, the study revealed that about 1 in 4 memory cells in a brain area called the hippocampus acts as a shared "hub" that links incoming and outgoing signals. A report on the findings was published in the journal Nature.

mix_items.title: Poor sleep health in adolescence linked to hospitalization and worse health in young adulthood
mix_items.summary: A new study to be presented at the SLEEP 2026 annual meeting found that poorer sleep health according to wrist actigraphy and insomnia symptoms during adolescence predicted a higher likelihood of an overnight hospital stay during young adulthood around seven years later, even after accounting for adolescent hospitalizations and sleep health.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally crack an “undruggable” pancreatic cancer target and nearly double survival
mix_items.summary: For decades, pancreatic cancer has been one of the most lethal cancers, with few effective treatment options. A new drug, daraxonrasib, targets the KRAS mutation that fuels most pancreatic tumors—something many scientists once thought couldn't be done. In a major clinical trial, the treatment nearly doubled survival for patients with advanced disease and reduced the risk of death by 60%.

mix_items.title: Majestic manta rays dive deep to survive storm events, data reveal
mix_items.summary: New research led by the University of the Sunshine Coast has found that reef manta rays are diving deep in storm events to find food and stay alive. As World Environment Day is celebrated around the globe on June 5, the findings offer hope for the future of a species listed as vulnerable to extinction.

mix_items.title: Short videos may hinder learning by fragmenting attention and memory, study finds
mix_items.summary: Recent technological advances and the introduction of new digital media platforms have dramatically changed how people learn and source information about topics that interest them. Some recent studies have found that while browsing online or scrolling down social media platforms, users tend to spend under one minute on average on individual videos.

mix_items.title: Goethe never knew this 40-million-year-old ant was hidden in his collection
mix_items.summary: Scientists examining amber from Goethe’s personal collection discovered three hidden fossil insects, including an extinct ant preserved in extraordinary detail. Advanced 3D imaging allowed researchers to see not only the ant’s outer features but also structures inside its body. The findings offer new clues about the species’ biology and suggest it likely built large nests in trees.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover the master clock that controls biological growth and development
mix_items.summary: A newly discovered genetic clock acts as the master timekeeper for development, orchestrating crucial bursts of gene activity throughout a worm’s growth. When the clock is disrupted, development stops, offering fresh clues about how growth-related disorders may arise.

mix_items.title: What went right this week: life-changing inventions, plus more
mix_items.summary: New life-changing inventions, a global triumph for renewables, and England’s first Dutch-style ‘cycle street’, plus more The post What went right this week: life-changing inventions, plus more appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why Ozempic and Wegovy weight loss eventually plateaus
mix_items.summary: New NIH research reveals that semaglutide sparks different responses inside appetite-controlling brain cells, offering fresh insight into why GLP-1 weight-loss drugs don’t work the same for everyone. Scientists also found a possible way to extend the drugs’ effects, potentially helping patients push past weight-loss plateaus.

mix_items.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
mix_items.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Am zis sa ies la aer. Am deschis geamul si am bifat.

satire_ro.title: CTP regretă că a plecat de la Digi! Cu banii din programul SAFE îşi făcea implant de păr
